1: Chapter 6 is set under Darius the Mede, who receives the kingdom after Belshazzar's fall (Daniel 5:31). The Darius figure is historically problematic: no Babylonian or Persian source names a “Darius the Mede” as a distinct king between Belshazzar and Cyrus. Proposed identifications include: (1) Gubaru/Gobryas, the general who captured Babylon for Cyrus and served as its governor; (2) Cyrus himself, with “Darius” as a throne-name; (3) an otherwise unrecorded official. The 120 satraps (rab-ravravan) of Daniel 6:1 is a large number — the Persian empire later had around 20-30 satraps under Darius I — but the number may reflect a transitional administrative arrangement immediately after the Babylonian conquest.
2: The three presidents (sarkeen, overseers) placed over the 120 satraps form a middle tier of administration. The Persian administrative structure included satrapal governors responsible to the crown, with oversight officials reducing corruption and ensuring revenue collection. Daniel's elevation to first among the three presidents reflects both his recognized competence (from decades of Babylonian court service) and possibly Darius's policy of retaining capable Babylonian bureaucrats for the transition. Persian practice of incorporating conquered elites into the administration is well-documented in the Achaemenid records.
3: Daniel's excellence above the other administrators is attributed to an excellent spirit (ruach yatira) — the same phrase used by Nebuchadnezzar (Daniel 4:8) and the queen-mother (Daniel 5:11-12). Over fifty years in royal courts, Daniel has maintained both professional excellence and spiritual integrity. The king's intention to set Daniel over the whole kingdom would make him effectively chief minister — analogous to Joseph under Pharaoh (Genesis 41:40-44). The parallel to Joseph is deliberate: exile, faithful service, elevation to second-in-command, the envy of court rivals.
4: The officials (sarkeen v'avadaya, presidents and satraps) seek a charge against Daniel. The verb yivqron (were seeking/examining) suggests a systematic investigation — they audit his administrative conduct. The finding that he is blameless (kol-illa v'shikhlul la-yikholon leshkhakha: no ground of complaint, no corruption) is the narrator's testimony to Daniel's integrity after fifty-plus years in pagan courts. The ancient world, like the modern, expected bureaucratic corruption; Daniel's unblemished record is presented as extraordinary and as a mark of YHWH's enabling.
5: The plotters conclude that the only possible charge against Daniel relates to the law of his God (dat elaheh). The religious test is the last resort of those who cannot find administrative grounds. The parallel to modern religious persecution is exact: when integrity cannot be attacked on professional grounds, religious practice becomes the target. The phrase din elaheh (law of his God) acknowledges that Daniel lives under a higher law — the plotters use his piety as the mechanism of his destruction.
6: The officials approach the king in a body — regashu v'amru (thronged/assembled and said to the king) — using the same verb (regash) that appears in Psalms 2:1 for the nations that conspire against YHWH and his anointed. The proposed decree is that for thirty days, prayer to any god or man except the king is punishable by the lions' den. Thirty days is a deliberate interval: long enough to force a confrontation with Daniel's regular prayer practice, short enough to be plausibly acceptable as a temporary gesture of imperial loyalty.
7: The Persian legal principle of the irrevocable royal decree appears here and in Esther 1:19 and Daniel 8:8 (the laws of the Medes and Persians do not alter). Classical sources (Diodorus Siculus, Plutarch) confirm this principle: Persian kings could not revoke their own decrees once written and sealed. This creates the narrative tension: the king wants to rescue Daniel but is legally bound. The irrevocability of Persian law is not just a plot device but a historical feature — one that makes the political trap against Daniel almost perfectly designed.
8: The establishment (qayam) of the decree uses the technical Aramaic term for a law made fixed and unalterable. The king signs (reshom khetava) — writes/engraves the document. The Aramaic phrase dat uqzira (the law and the limit/decree) uses two terms for legal binding: dat (law/regulation) and qzer (decree/decision). The sealing with the royal seal (khatam) makes it legally effective and irrevocable. The narrative detail that the king signs without apparently realizing that Daniel — whom he favors — will be caught by the law is a study in courtly manipulation.
9: The decree is signed and sealed — an irreversible legal fact. The Aramaic perfect tense (reshim ketaba) indicates completed action. The narrative pauses to note the legal finality before turning to Daniel's response. The court's trap has been successfully set. The contrast with the previous chapter is striking: Belshazzar's feast was disrupted by divine intervention from outside; here the divine intervention will come in response to a believer's prayer from inside the trap. The difference between Daniel 5 and Daniel 6 is the difference between Belshazzar's defiant ignorance and Daniel's faithful knowledge.
10: Daniel's response to the new law: he continues his practice unchanged. The three-times-daily prayer practice (zimnin tlata b'yoma, three times a day) facing Jerusalem corresponds to the Psalms 55:17 practice (“Evening and morning and at noon I utter my complaint”) and the later Jewish liturgical pattern of shacharit (morning), minchah (afternoon), and arvit (evening) prayers. Praying toward Jerusalem was established in Solomon's temple-dedication prayer (1 Kings 8:44-48) for use in exile — Daniel practices exactly what Solomon's prayer prescribed. The open windows emphasize deliberateness: he does not pray in secret to avoid detection.
11: The officials (gavrayya ilein, those men) assemble as a group to catch Daniel in the act — the Aramaic haragshu (rushed/assembled together) uses the same verb as Daniel 6:6 and Psalms 2:1. The tableau is deliberate: Daniel is found baeh (in the act of) praying and seeking mercy (mitcanan) before his God. The word mitcanan (seeking mercy/grace) is the Aramaic form of the Hebrew techinah — the supplicatory prayer that invokes YHWH's covenant mercy. Daniel is arrested precisely in the act of covenant prayer.
12: The officials first remind the king of his own decree before naming Daniel — a rhetorical strategy to bind the king before he can object. Hala-la dat reshamt (did you not establish a law?) forces the king to affirm the decree in principle before hearing its application to Daniel. The narrative technique is the same as Nathan's parable to David (2 Samuel 12:1-7): establish the principle, then apply it. The king cannot disavow his decree without admitting it was foolish — he is caught in the legal trap as surely as Daniel.
13: The accusers name Daniel explicitly: di min-benei galuta di-yehud (who is from the exiles of Judah) — the ethnic identifier is designed to minimize Daniel's status. He is not a Persian nobleman but an exile, a captured foreigner. The charge: la sam ʿalakh malka taʿam (does not pay attention to you, O king) — framing his prayer as political disloyalty. The three-times-daily prayer is the evidence. The political framing of religious faithfulness as civil insubordination is a technique that recurs throughout Jewish and Christian history.
14: The king's distress (beish ʿaloh saggi, greatly displeased with himself) and his effort to rescue Daniel reveal the narrative irony: the man who signed the death-decree without thinking is now the one most distressed by its application. The Aramaic phrase ad-maʿal shemsha havah mshatdal leshezavuteh (until sunset he was striving to deliver him) shows protracted effort — the entire day is spent in legal deliberation trying to find a loophole. The irony underscores a theme Daniel pursues: human legal systems trap their makers; only YHWH's law is perfectly just.
15: The officials enforce the legal trap: dat Madai u-Pharas hi (the law of the Medes and Persians is) that no injunction the king establishes can be changed. The phrase la lehaʿalah (cannot be altered) is the same principle from Esther 1:19 and Daniel 8:8. The officials have perfectly designed their trap: the law's irrevocability, which was normally a guarantee of royal authority, has been turned into a weapon against the king's own will. The empire's greatest strength — the binding permanence of royal decrees — becomes the instrument of injustice.
16: The lions' den (gob areyavata, pit of lions) is attested as an execution device in ancient Mesopotamia — Ashurbanipal's reliefs depict lion-hunting pits, and literary evidence suggests royal lions were kept for both sport and punishment. The king's farewell to Daniel — elahakh di ant paleikh-leh hu yeshizevinnakh (your God whom you serve continuously, he will deliver you) — is a remarkable statement of faith from a pagan king. Whether sincere or resigned, it expresses the book's theological thesis: the god Daniel serves is the one with power to save, even from lions.
17: The sealing of the stone over the den's mouth with the king's seal and the nobles' seals: hatam b'ʿizqateh di-malka uvʿizqath rabrevaneh (sealed with the ring of the king and with the ring of his nobles). The multiple seals prevent the king himself from releasing Daniel unofficially — even the king is bound. The detail is legally precise: the seal witnesses prevent any party from acting unilaterally. The narrative irony: the most thoroughly sealed, officially witnessed human containment becomes the theater of divine deliverance.
18: Darius's night of fasting: tavata la-atal qodamoh (food was not brought before him) and dakhawin la-atal qodamoh (diversions were not brought before him). The Hebrew/Aramaic root dakhava (concubines? musical instruments? food?) is uncertain — possibly entertainment or musical performance. The sleeplessness (shnatech addat ʿaloh, his sleep fled from him) is the physical expression of moral distress: the king who was legally bound cannot escape the emotional consequence of his legal trap.
19: The king goes to the den at first light — b'shafarpara b'nohora (at the break of dawn, in the light). The compound expression uses both the Aramaic term for dawn-light (shafar, beauty/brightness) and the noun nohora (light). The urgency — going at the very first light — shows that the king has not resigned himself to Daniel's death but is going to see whether a miracle has occurred. The Aramaic behitbehala azel (went in haste/distress) conveys his emotional state throughout the journey.
20: The king's cry at the den's entrance: Daniel ʿaved elaha khaya (Daniel, servant of the living God). The epithet el khaya (living God) is the standard Hebrew/Aramaic contrast with inert idols — a living God who can intervene versus gods of gold and silver who cannot (cf. Jeremiah 10:10; Deuteronomy 5:26). The question — elahakh di ant paleikh-leh bithidira hitsyl-lakh min-areyavata (your God whom you serve continuously, has he been able to deliver you from the lions?) — uses the word yakhil (able) from ch. Daniel 3:17. The God of Daniel is consistently asked whether he is able.
21: Daniel's response opens with the court formula: malka lealam kheyey (O king, live forever). Even from inside the lions' den, Daniel observes the courtly protocol — a detail that emphasizes his composure and dignity. The response follows the pattern of Daniel 3: faithfulness brings death-threat, death-threat is survived, survivor addresses the king with dignity. The literary parallel between the fiery furnace (Daniel 3) and the lions' den (Daniel 6) is intentional — both chapters end with Gentile kings issuing doxologies honoring YHWH.
22: Daniel's explanation of his deliverance: elahi shallach malʾakheh v-semar pum areyavata (my God sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths). The angel (malʾakh) is unnamed — different from Gabriel in Daniel 8-9. The reason for his deliverance is stated in two parallel clauses: di qodamoh zakit ezhtakhat li (because before him I was found innocent) and v-af kadamakh malka khavala la avedeth (and also before you, O king, I have done no harm). The twin innocence — before God and before the king — is Daniel's defense. He has not actually violated the Persian law's legitimate basis; he violated only its illegitimate misuse.
23: Daniel brought out: v-kol-divin la-eshtkakhath beh (and no kind of harm was found in him). The verification of no injury — a formal physical inspection — parallels the inspection of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego after the furnace (Daniel 3:27: not singed, no smell of fire). Both deliverance narratives require this formal verification by witnesses. The reason given for the deliverance is theological and anthropological: di-hemin b-elaheh (because he trusted/believed in his God). The verb hemin (believed/trusted) is the Aramaic form of aman (to be faithful/believe) — the root of amen.
24: The accusers cast into the lions' den with their families — a distinctively ANE punishment that strikes modern readers as harsh. The collective punishment of families for individual crimes was standard Babylonian and Persian practice (cf. Daniel 2:5, Daniel 3:29, and Esther's parallel plotters' fate). The narrative detail that the lions overpower them before they reach the bottom of the pit (v-lehon tarou areyavata b-gov garimayyon daqu) serves to validate the miracle: the lions were genuinely dangerous and would have killed Daniel had YHWH not intervened. The lions' behavior was supernaturally restrained, not naturally gentle.
25: Darius's letter to all peoples, nations, and languages uses the same triadic formula as Nebuchadnezzar's letters in Daniel 3-4. The pattern of Gentile kings issuing imperial doxologies to YHWH after experiencing his power is a structural feature of Daniel's court narratives (Nebuchadnezzar in Daniel 2:47; Daniel 3:28-29; Daniel 4:34-35; Darius here). The letters create a literary record of Gentile testimony to YHWH — the book of Daniel itself becomes a collection of such testimonies, a kind of prophetic argument that YHWH is the God of all nations.
26: Darius's decree to honor Daniel's God: min-kodamai sim teʿem di b-khol-shaltan malkhuti yehoon zaʿin v-daylin qodam elaheh di-Daniel (from before me a decree is set that in all the dominion of my kingdom men tremble and fear before the God of Daniel). The language echoes Nebuchadnezzar's similar decree in Daniel 3:29. YHWH is identified with specific attributes: hu elaha khayya v-qayam leʿalmin (he is the living God who endures forever) — contrasting him with the Babylonian and Persian gods who exist in statues and temples. The eternal-permanence claim for YHWH echoes the same claim Daniel challenged the empires to make for themselves and failed.
27: The doxology's content: mesheziv u-metsiyl v-aved aten v-mofteen bishemayya u-varʿa (delivering and rescuing and working signs and wonders in heaven and earth). The two verbs of deliverance (mesheziv, delivering; metsiyl, rescuing) are the same pair used throughout Daniel 3-6 for miraculous intervention. The phrase bishemayya u-varʿa (in heaven and earth) claims universal scope for YHWH's power — not a local deity but the ruler of both realms. The specific application: di sheziv l-Daniel min-yad areyavata (who delivered Daniel from the hand of the lions) grounds the cosmic claim in the specific historical event.
28: The closing summary: Daniel prospered (tzlach) in the reign of Darius and in the reign of Cyrus the Persian. The mention of Cyrus closes the narrative frame: Cyrus is the king who issued the edict allowing the Jews to return from exile (Ezra 1:1-4; Isaiah 45:1). Daniel, who was taken into exile at the beginning of the Babylonian period, survives to see the Cyrus edict — the full arc of the exile from its beginning to its end is spanned in Daniel's lifetime. The final verse signals that the political story of exile is ending, setting the stage for the prophetic visions of Daniel 7-12 about what comes after.
